predictions.description: file-exists calibrated on the SAME xlsx path across both polarities: false before anything ran (clean slate), true right after xl-save-as-xlsx — and the harness's own file_exists confirms the binary artifact physically survives the run.
xl-create-new-workbook(numberOfSheets=2) named its sheets Sheet1, Sheet2 — read back by a different action (xl-get-sheet-names) and asserted as an exact array.
The reopened A1 equalsExact check is token-built against {var:_sgtSessionId} (the DSL cannot express that value), so it lives in-bot and reports via the crumbs list.
Sheet2!B2=42 written before save-as-xlsx came back through an independent xl-open-existing-workbook into a SECOND variable — sheet routing and the numeric cell both survive the binary round-trip.

After mutating the reopened workbook (A2) and xl-save (in place — filePath inherited from the open step), a THIRD independent open sees the mutation: xl-save physically rewrote the file, not just the variable.

xl-save-as-xlsx with replaceExisting=true really replaced the file: a fresh open reads the first workbook's new A1 content (the wb2 lineage's A2 write was clobbered along with the rest of that file version).
xl-open-existing-workbook on a .csv routes through ExcelJS's csv reader: B2 of the parsed grid is the second field of the second row.

Four buried failures fired (xl-save with no filePath, save-as onto an existing file with replaceExisting=false, opening a .xls, reading a CLOSED workbook) — smoke.expectErrorCount=0 proves they were buried, and the closed-workbook read failed BEFORE touching its result variable (sentinel intact). The closed-read failure is also the witness that xl-close-workbook actually cleared the variable.
